title: Healthy Fats Guide
content:
HEALTHY FATS TIP #1
Cook your vegetables in a
healthy fat source for added
nutrients and to make them
taste delicious!HEALTHY FATSguide to
Fats are crucial for numerous body functions and structures. For example, the fat in our
food helps us absorb the fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K, helps improve the taste and
enjoyment of food, increases satiety, and helps regulate the speed at which we digest
food. 
FATS & METABOLISM
When you’re building a fire, one important component is the kindling. It burns bright and
intense, but for a short period of time. This is like the carbohydrate energy source. It’s
quickly digested, and quickly used. The other component of a fire is the long, slow
burning log. It takes a bit to get going, but once it does, it can sustain the fire for hours.
This is like the energy source of fats. 
They can sustain us in endurance activities and keep our
blood sugar stable between meals. Kindling and the big
logs are both important to keeping a fire going, just as
carbohydrates, and fats, are both important components
of a healthy metabolism.
It also provides an important source of high
caloric energy that is ideal for long, low-
intensity activity. Dietary fats are also
needed to build cell membranes and certain
hormones, and they serve as a protective
lining for the organs of the body.
Omega-6s are required for the pro-inflammatory response like helping to clear
infection, omega-3s are required to help resolve that inflammation when the acute
phase is over. Ideally, the ratio between the omega-6s and omega-3s should be
between 1:1 and 4:1.
Trying to hit a perfect omega-6 to omega-3 balance can be difficult, and it’s much
easier – and still effective – to start by incorporating more high-quality omega-3 and 6
fatty acid.OMEGA-3 AND OMEGA-6 FATTY ACIDS
Margarine
The manufacturing process required to turn industrial crops into usable oil involves many steps,
which often include high heat (which can lead to oxidation), solvent-based extraction, bleaching
and deodorizing, and addition of colorants. 
Hydrogenated and man-made trans-fats
Man-made trans-fats have been shown to increase risk of cardiovascular issues,
insulin resistance, diabetes, and cancer. These industrial oils are unnatural,
unhealthy, and unnecessary.Industrial seed oils like canola, soybean, cottonseed, corn, and
vegetable oilsHEALTHY FATS TIP #2
Eat fatty cuts of
pasture raised meat
for the most flavor and
nutrients.Managing inflammation in the body
Supporting healthy cells
Maintaining optimal cognitive function
FATS TO AVOID
Avoid man-made and highly refined fats
when possible, including:HAVING THE RIGHT RATIO IS CRITICAL FOR:HEALTHY FATSguide to
EAT A 
OF HEALTHY FATS:variety
Pistachios,
walnuts, pumpkin
seeds, sunflower
seeds and their
respective cold-
pressed oils
Organic peanut
butter, avocado,
and organic tofuOily fish like
salmon, herring,
mackerel, sardines,
and anchovies
Pasture-raised egg
yolks
Grass-fed beef,
oysters, walnuts,
and chia seedsHEALTHY FATS TIP #3
Fatty fish like wild caught
salmon is an excellent
source of the essential
long-chain omega-3 fatty
acids EPA and DHA.
Grass-fed animals
Pasture-raised
eggs
Sustainably raised
seafoodOrganic and
unrefined cold-
pressed oils, such as:
Coconut oilAlpha-linolenic acid (ALA) is found mainly in
plant foods
DHA and EPA are found in certain fatty fish, grass-fed
meats and pastured poultry and eggs, as well as in some
types of marine algaeTHE THREE MAIN TYPES OF OMEGA-3 FATTY ACIDS:HEALTHY FATSguide to
ANIMAL FATS PLANT FATS OMEGA-6 OMEGA-3

olive oil

title: Digestion Guide
content:
Guide to DigestionA SIMPLE & PRACTICAL
DIGESTION OVERVIEW
The human body and its systems are absolutely
fascinating! We truly believe that when we bring a
little more awareness and a touch of awe and
wonder to the table (literally!), we will begin to
experience an increase of wellbeing. 
The digestive system begins working the moment
that we approach a meal - even just the thought of
what we want to eat gets our system in gear. 
Did you know that HOW you eat is as important as
WHAT you eat? This guide will provide you with
some practical, simple ways to shift the way you eat
so that your digestive system can function optimally.
© Nutritional Therapy Association, Inc.
Take time to sit down while you’re eating
Set a timer for one minute of slow, deep
breathing, or at least take 5 deep breaths
before you begin to eat
Challenge yourself to eat in a calm, relaxed
state
Begin a meal with a gratitude practice
Take time to savor the flavors of your meal
Imagine your body digesting your foodTHE BRAIN & BODY CONNECTION
HOW TO PRACTICALLY INCREASE YOUR BODY’S
PARASYMPATHETIC TONE:
© Nutritional Therapy Association, Inc.
MINDFUL EATING PRACTICES
HERE ARE SOME SIMPLE TIPS TO IMPLEMENT
AT YOUR NEXT MEAL:
© Nutritional Therapy Association, Inc.Chew your food 15-30 times per bite, or
until it’s the consistency of a smoothie.
When struggling with indigestion, it is often
more comfortable and beneficial to eat solid
foods and chew them well than to eat liquid
meals as mastication helps us activate later
steps in digestion and increases salivary
amylase.
Consume foods with bitter flavors, this
helps to prime your entire digestive tract!
Consider using traditional digestive bitters
with your meals.HOW you eat is just as important as WHAT you eat. 
DIGESTIVE BITTERS 
HERE ARE SOME EASY WAYS TO ADD BITTERS
TO YOUR ROUTINE:
© Nutritional Therapy Association, Inc.Start your meal with a salad containing
bitter greens like kale, arugula, or dandelion
leaves. Artichokes, radicchio, and endives
make excellent bitter additions too!
Take some apple cider vinegar or lemon
juice prior to a meal, diluted with a bit of
water if needed. You can also use either of
these with some olive oil to dress a salad.
Enjoy bitter herbs in a tincture. You can
make or purchase digestive bitters in the
form of a tincture with herbs like gentian,
burdock, ginger, and orange peel. Simple
add a dropper or two to a glass of water
and enjoy about 20 minutes prior to eating.Starting a meal with bitter flavors will get your
digestive juices flowing and ready for the work ahead!

title: Herbal Recipe Guide
content:
AN HERBAL
RECIPE GUIDE
recipes to delight
Feminini-Tea
🫖 TEA BLEND
¼ cup each, Hawthorn berry and
leaf/flower 
½ cup Rooibos tea 
2 tablespoons Hibiscus 
½ cup Linden leaf
2 tablespoons Rose hips 
½ cup Nettle 
1 cup Red raspberry leaf 
1 cup Red clover 
DIRECTIONS:
Blend herbs together and store in a jar.
Steep 1-2 tablespoons of tea blend in 8
oz of just boiled water for 5 minutes for
an enjoyable cup and 30 minutes for a
more medicinal brew. 

Golden Milk
HERBAL POWDER BLEND
3 tablespoons Organic ground tumeric
2 teaspoons Organic ground ginger 
2 teaspoons Organic ground cinnamon 
½ teaspoon Organic ground black
pepper
¼ teaspoon Organic ground cardamom 
GOLDEN MILK
1 teaspoon Herb Powder Blend
1 cup milk of choice
Sweetener of choice, to taste
DIRECTIONS:
Whisk powder and milk in sauce pan. Heat
on medium-low heat for 10 minutes. Add
sweetener and enjoy!

Immunity Chai
BATCH RECIPE
2 tablespoons Dried ginger root
2 tablespoons Dried orange peel 
1 tablespoon Cinnamon chips 
1 teaspoon Peppercorns
½ teaspoon Hulled cardamom or two
crushed cardamom
¼ teaspoon cloves (about 3-5 cloves)
10-20 grams Astragalus
6-9 grams sliced Reishi
6 cups Water
DIRECTIONS:
Place all ingredients in a pan. Bring to boil,
then lower heat. Cover and simmer for one
hour. Strain. Add milk and honey, if
desired. 

Cocoa Bliss
TEA BLEND
½ cup tablespoons Cacao shells
¼ cup Cinnamon chips 
4 Vanilla beans, chopped
DIRECTIONS:
Steep 2 tablespoons of tea blend in
8oz of water for 10 minutes. Strain and
sweeten as desired. 

Herbal Salt Blends
BLEND ONE:
Paprika (2 parts)
Garlic (2 parts)
Pepper (1/2 part)
Thyme (1 part)
Celtic Sea Salt (1 or 2 parts)
BLEND TWO:
Parsley (2 parts)
Oregano (1 part)
Chives (2 parts)
Turmeric (1/2 part)
Bay (1 part)
Celtic Sea Salt (1 or 2 parts)
DIRECTIONS:
Mix the herbs and salt together. Use a
mortar and pestle if a fine grind is desired.
Store in a sprinkle top jar.

GlucoBalance
VINEGAR MIXTURE:
½ cup gymnema leaf
2 Tbsp orange peel
½ cup peppermint
2 Tbsp cinnamon
2 Tbsp fenugreek
½ cup gentian root
¼ cup bupleurum
Apple cider vinegar to cover
ALCOHOL MIXTURE:
¼ cup gymnema leaf
1 Tbsp orange peel
¼ cup peppermint
1 Tbsp cinnamon
1 Tbsp fenugreek
¼ cup gentian root
2 Tbsp bupleurum
Vodka to cover

DI R ECTI O N S:
Combine the herbs and vinegar in
one jar and the herbs and alcohol
in another. Keep covered in a dark
place and let sit for 4-6 weeks for
vinegar, 6-8 weeks for alcohol.
Strain and combine the liquids to
create your tincture. Take a half or
a full dropper in 8 ounces of water
prior to meals.GlucoBalance

Digestive Bitters
VINEGAR MIXTURE:
½ cup Gentian root
½ cup Dandelion root
½ cup Burdock root
½ cup Artichoke leaf
2 tablespoons Fennel Seeds
2 tablespoons Orange peel
2 tablespoons Ginger root 
2-3 cups Apple cider vinegar
ALCOHOL MIXTURE:
¼ cup Gentian root
¼ cup Dandelion root
¼ cup Burdock root
¼ cup Artichoke leaf
1 tablespoons Fennel Seeds
1 tablespoons Orange peel
1 tablespoons Ginger root 
Vodka to cover

title: Herbal Recipe Guide
content:
Digestive Bitters
VINEGAR DIRECTIONS:
Combine all ingredients in a jar
and cover with vinegar. Let sit for
a few hours and then top off with
vinegar. Let sit covered in a dark
place for 4-6 weeks. Strain. Can
use as is or combine with alcohol
mixture. 
ALCOHOL DIRECTIONS:
Combine all ingredients in a jar
and cover with vodka. Let sit
covered in a dark place for 6-8
weeks. Strain. Mix alcohol
mixture with the vinegar mixture. 

Herbal Oil
CALENDULA OR
CHAMOMILE OIL
3 ounces Powdered, dried
calendula or chamomile
21 ounces Jojoba oil 
2 ounces Alcohol (80-100 proof
vodka)
DIRECTIONS:
Rehydrate herbs overnight in alcohol
for 8 hours in a covered container.
blend rehydrated powder with oil for 5
minutes. For 48 hours, blend mixture
for 5 minutes at least 5 times a day.
Strain.

Body Butter
FIRM OR WHIPPED
2 ounces Herb infused oil
1 tablespoon Beeswax 
1 tablespoon cocoa butter
DIRECTIONS:
Gently melt ingredients together in a
double boiler. For firm, simply pour into
a jar and let cool. For whipped, cool for
10 minutes, then chill in the fridge for 1
hour. whip with a mixer for 5 minutes. 

2 tablespoons Lavender
2 tablespoons Chamomile 
2 tablespoons Elderflower 
8 cups Water
DI R ECTI O N S:
Bring water to a boil. Add herbs to a
bowl, cover with hot water. Lean over
the bowl, draping a towel over you head
to form a tent over the bowl and trap the
steam. Enjoy for 5-15 minutes. Floral Facial Steam

2 tablespoons Nettle
2 tablespoons Rhassoul clay 
2 tablespoons Chamomile 
2 tablespoons Powdered rose or
lavender
Honey and Rosehip seed oil for
application. 
DI R ECTI O N S:
Combine the dry ingredients and store
in a jar. 
To make the face mask, combine 1
tablespoon of the dry mixture with ½
teaspoon rosehip seed oil and ½
teaspoon honey. Add water if it is
difficult to spread. Massage on the face
and let sit for 10-15 minutes. Nettle Face Mask

¾ cup herb infused oil*
1 cup beeswax pellets
1 cup shea butter
1 tablespoon honey
DI R ECTI O N S:
Combine all ingredients in a double
boiler over low heat, stirring
occasionally until melted. Pour into
molds and let cool. Makes 8 lotion bars.
*Calendula, chamomile, spruce, plantain, or
Saint John’s Wort oil are great choices. You
can purchase oils at Mountain Rose Herbs
or make your own with this free workshop. Lotion Bars

AR TI CH O KES
Artichoke leaf promotes bile production,
improves cholesterol levels, improves
symptoms of indigestion, and its better
compounds support healthy digestion.
ASTR AG ALUS
Sweet and slightly warming, astragalus
is used to nourish and build, especially
for those with low energy. It improves
digestion, protects against colds and
flu, it’s antiviral, may improve insulin
resistance, protects the heart and lungs,
reduces night sweats, supports kidney
health, supports wound healing, and is
generally supportive for immune
function.Herb Facts

title: Herbal Recipe Guide
content:
BUPLEUR UM
Bupleurum is hepatoprotective,
protecting the liver, reducing liver
stagnation, and decreasing inflammation.
It is an immunomodulator, a nervous
system tonic, and provides general
nourishment and restoration of balance
for organs and systems. 
BUR DO CK
Burdock root supports the health of the
sebaceous glands that secrete sebum to
lubricate and moisturize the skin and
hair. It is antimicrobial, supports the
urinary system, is high in inulin to support
a healthy gut microbiome, and supports
the absorption and utilization of lipids. 
CACAO
Cacao boosts energy , improves mood,
reduces anxiety, supports hearth health,
cognitive function, reduces
hypertension, increases insulin
sensitivity, high in antioxidants,
flavonoids, and inflammation modulating. 

CALEN DULA
Calendula shines for supporting skin
health and mucus membranes. It
supports wound healing, reduces
inflammation, improves immune
response, protects from radiation,
relieves rashes, itching, and burns,
decreases scar tissue, modulates
inflammation, decreases dandruff, is high
in antioxidants and flavonoids, supports
gut healing, postpartum recovery, helps
clear a stagnant liver and stimulates
lymphatic drainage. 
CAR DAM O M
Cardamom increases the appetite,
enhances digestion, and stimulates
circulation throughout the digestive
tract. It supports the relief of nausea,
vomiting, morning sickness, belching,
digestive cramping, gas, bloating,
flatulence, indigestion, diarrhea, colic,
and digestive headaches. It improves fat
digestion and acts as a gentle laxative. It
supports in instances of respiratory
infection, sore throat, coughs, and is
warming without being overheating. 

CH AM O M I LE
With chamomile, it is important to
remember that gentle does not mean
weak. This flower can soothe the nervous
system, promote healthy digestion,
relieve muscle tension, and modulate the
immune system. It helps relieve stress
and anxiety and promotes healthy sleep,
especially when used long-term. It helps
the nervous system get into a
parasympathetic state. It supports
reduction of menstrual cramps and
headaches associated with tension. It
supports fevers by letting heat naturally
release through the skin, improves fat
digestion, and relieves itching and
inflammation for the skin.

CI N N AM O N
Cinnamon is warming and can improve
circulation, move stagnant digestion,
supports indigestion, gas, and cramping,
stokes the metabolic fire, anti-microbial,
supports oral health, anti-inflammatory,
lowers blood pressure, lowers blood
glucose levels, warming and nourishing
with colds and flu that involve
chills/shivers, decrease inflammation in
situations like rheumatoid arthritis, and
supports respiratory health. 
CLO VE
Clove is a powerful analgesic that can
relieve pain. it is very commonly used for
toothaches and gum issues. it can
accelerate circulation, is antiparasitic,
can help preserve food, relieves stomach
aches, acts as an expectorant, is
antimicrobial, and can relieve gas and
bloating.

title: Herbal Recipe Guide
content:
DAN DELI O N
Dandelion roots are nutrient dense, with
lots of vitamins, minerals, and
phytonutrients. They are also a great
source of inulin, which can help restore a
healthy gut microbiome. It supports
improvement in instances of congested
liver, swollen gallbladder,
congested/thick bile, gallstone
formation, and constipation. By relieving
liver stagnation, it supports improved fat
digestion and absorption, proper
balance of estrogen, improved PMS
symptoms, and regulation of bowel
movements. It decreases inflammation
and protects healthy cells. It has been
used to support arthritis and has
demonstrated effectiveness against
certain kinds of cancer. 

ELDER FLO W ER
Elderflower excels in supporting fever
when the person is hot, but is not
sweating. It opens capillaries and dispels
heat. When taken at the onset of a cold
or flu, it can shorten the duration of
sickness. It’s ability to support sweating
is also a natural detoxification support for
skin. It supports soothing of inflamed,
irritated skin and rashes, and may protect
against UV rays. They are anti-
inflammatory and support eye,
cardiovascular, and respiratory health. 
FEN N EL
Fennel supports the health of the
digestive system in many ways. It
improves gas, bloating, nausea,
constipation, spasms, and constipation.
It is antibacterial and anti-inflammatory.
It helps break up mucous stuck in the
lungs and can soothe dry coughs. 

FEN UG R EEK
Fenugreek has been shown to lower
blood glucose levels and cholesterol
levels. It has mild laxative effect and
helps support healthy digestive function.
It is antibacterial, anti-inflammatory,
supports wound healing, and can be
used to promote lactation in nursing
mothers. 
G EN TI AN
Gentian root stimulates HCL and bile
secretion, tones the digestive tract, and
is considered a key herb for digestive
weakness of any kind. It can stimulate
appetite, reduce bloating, diarrhea, and
heartburn. It supports liver health and
may support the body in assimilating
nutrients. 

G I N G ER
Fresh ginger is warm, dried ginger is hot.
It is anti-inflammatory, supports
circulation, relieves inflammation-based
pain in situations like arthritis or muscle
pain after workouts, moves stagnation all
throughout the body, helps alleviate
menstrual cramps, supports migraine
relief, relieves congestion, sore throat,
and provides general support for colds
and flu, helps relieve gas, bloating,
nausea, constipation, and helps with
recovery from food poisoning. Ginger
reduces blood glucose levels and
modulates cholesterol. It fights infection
including ear infections, fungal
infections, and stomach bugs. It is
known as a synergist and is often
included in formulations because it helps
circulate and distribute other herbs
throughout the body.

title: Herbal Recipe Guide
content:
G YM N EM A
Gymnema is primarily used as an
antidiabetic herb. It can improve insulin
secretion and reduce blood sugar levels
as well as increase the activity of
enzymes that are responsible for glucose
uptake and usage. It is also a diuretic
and its astringent properties support
digestive health. 
H AW TH O R N
Hawthorn is anti-hypertensive, reduces
cholesterol, and nourishes and protects
the heart. It is rich in flavonoids and is
anti-inflammatory. It is a relaxing nervine
known for supporting the emotional side
of the heart during times of grief and
heartbreak. Historically it was used to
support digestion and brings relief for
symptoms of indigestion. 

H I BI SCUS
Hibiscus is a demulcent herb that helps
relieve dry mucus membranes and
soothes parched tissues. it is high in
polyphenols that support mitochondrial
function, energy levels, and
cardiovascular health. It can reduce
blood pressure, lower triglycerides,
decrease oxidative stress, and is a
powerful antioxidant. It has even been
used to support healthy iron levels in
anemic people. it has been shown to
effectively kill cancer cells and increase
apoptosis. Hibiscus is also used to
support chronic coughs, kidney stones,
and inflammatory skin conditions.

LAVEN DER
Lavender relieves stress, promotes sleep,
and improves mood. It reduces anxiety
and can help people come out of the fog
of stagnant depression. It is antiseptic,
antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory,
which makes it an excellent herb for
healing wounds and skin issues. It has
even demonstrated effectiveness against
several parasites and candida yeast
overgrowth. Lavender can reduce our
sense of pain, even in intense situations
like labor or kidney stones. It also
provides gentle digestive support.

LI N DEN
Linden is relaxing, demulcent, cooling
herb that supports in times of excess
heat, stimulation, and dryness. it is a
relaxing nervine that supports the heart,
relieves tension, reduces blood pressure,
and can support decreased anxiety,
tension headaches, menstrual cramps,
muscle cramps, and restlessness. It helps
heal dry rashes and protects mucus
membranes, relieves stagnant digestion,
soothes indigestion, and relieves
diarrhea. As a vasodilator, it also
improves circulation, modulates the
immune system, and helps shorten the
duration of viral infections.

N ETTLE
Nettle is nutrient dense with a lot of
vitamins, minerals, and phytonutrients. It
strengthens bones, hair, nails, teeth, and
supports healthy energy level. It can
boost iron levels, relieve muscle cramps
and fill in the gaps in case of nutrient
deficiency. It decreases seasonal allergy
symptoms, supports lower blood glucose
level and reduced markers for diabetes,
decreases arthritis markers and
symptoms, and is a mild diuretic that can
tonify and support the urinary system.
O R AN G E
Orange peel contains much more
enzymes, flavonoids, and phytonutrients
than the actual fruit of the orange. It is a
bit bitter, which can also help stimulate
digestion.

title: Herbal Recipe Guide
content:
PEPPER
Black pepper is spicy and drying. It helps
move stagnant fluids, increases
production and movement of mucus
through the lungs and sinuses, increases
circulation, supports digestion and
improves transit time, supports the fever
process when chills are present, helps
relieve symptoms of arthritis, and
increases the bioavailability of other herb
and food constituents. It modulates the
immune system and shows promise as a
potential cancer protectant. 
PEPPER M I N T
Peppermint is a prime herb for digestion.
It can relieve a stomach ache, settle a
nervous stomach, and relieve gas and
bloating. It helps soothe pain from
headaches and menstrual cramps, calms
itching and inflammation, opens the
pores to reduce fever, stimulates energy,
and breaks up congestion in the lungs. 

RASPBERRY LEAF
Raspberry leaf is astringent and helps
tighten and tone tissues. it helps reduce
inflammation, soothe sore throats,
improves gum health, and supports the
upper respiratory tract. It tones the
uterus, relieves menstrual cramps, and
supports postpartum recovery. They are
also a good source of magnesium and
are rich in flavonoids and tannins. 
RED CLOVER
Red clover is an alterative, which is an
herb that helps the body eliminate
metabolic wastes. It supports lymphatic
movement and the liver’s detoxification
processes. It supports skin health, and
has been used in historical anti-cancer
remedies. It’s known for supporting
hormonal imbalances and supports with
issues of infertility and PMS. It is rich in
phytonutrients, supports relief from
spasmodic coughs like whooping cough,
and has been shown to downregulate
chronic inflammation, increase insulin
sensitivity, and support healthy
cholesterol levels. 

R O SEH I P
Rosehip is supportive of the
cardiovascular system and improves both
blood pressure and cholesterol levels.
They are high bioflavonoids and
antioxidants, modulate inflammation,
decrease pain and are rich in vitamin C. 
TUR M ER I C
Turmeric is warming and very drying.
Preparing with liquid and fat helps to
balance its drying qualities. It modulates
inflammation, heals the digestive tracts,
stimulates glutathione (supports the
body’s natural detoxification process),
clears stagnant digestion, tightens and
tones mucosal membranes, promotes
bile secretion, antioxidant, supports
heart health, is cancer protective,
supports musculoskeletal health,
strengthens cognitive function, supports
skin and scalp conditions, may support
the prevention of diabetes, and its blood
moving properties can alleviate
symptoms of PMS

VAN I LLA
Vanilla is both a stimulating and relaxing
nervine, so it is a fabulous option in
nervous system tonics. It has also been
used for stomach pains and coughs
historically.
